name: Tai Sui
description: Tai Sui, also known as the Grand Duke of Jupiter or the Star God of Age, is a complex and multifaceted deity in Chinese folk religion and astrology. He is not a single entity, but rather a collective of sixty different deities, each ruling over one year in the sexagenary cycle of the Chinese calendar. This cycle combines the ten Heavenly Stems and twelve Earthly Branches, creating a unique combination for each year. The Tai Sui's influence is believed to be particularly potent, affecting fortune, health, and general well-being. Disrespecting or contradicting the Tai Sui of the current year is thought to bring misfortune, emphasizing the importance of understanding and appeasing this celestial force through rituals, offerings, and careful planning of activities. The concept of Tai Sui reflects the Chinese worldview of a universe governed by cycles and the interconnectedness of celestial and earthly affairs. Temples dedicated to Tai Sui are common, where devotees seek blessings and protection against negative influences.
subtitle: Grand Duke of Jupiter

keyMyths.title: The Sixty Generals
keyMyths.description: The Tai Sui are not one entity but a collective of sixty generals, each ruling over a specific year in the sixty-year cycle. These generals are often depicted as military figures, reflecting their role in overseeing the affairs of the year and protecting against misfortune. Each general has their own name, origin story, and attributes, contributing to the rich tapestry of beliefs surrounding the Tai Sui. Their individual stories are often linked to historical figures known for their loyalty and righteousness.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E. T. C. (1922). *Myths and Legends of China*.

keyMyths.title: Offending Tai Sui
keyMyths.description: It is believed that actions which 'offend' the Tai Sui of the current year can bring bad luck. This 'offense' can manifest in various ways, such as facing the direction opposite to the Tai Sui's position when undertaking important activities like building or moving house. Geomancers and Feng Shui practitioners are often consulted to determine the Tai Sui's location and advise on how to avoid offending him. Specific rituals and offerings are also performed to appease the Tai Sui and mitigate potential negative effects.
keyMyths.source: Freedman, M. (1979). *Religion and Ritual: Chinese Society*.

keyMyths.title: Appeasing Tai Sui
keyMyths.description: To avoid misfortune, devotees often take measures to appease the Tai Sui. This can involve visiting temples dedicated to the Tai Sui, offering prayers and incense, and performing specific rituals. Wearing jade pendants or amulets depicting the Tai Sui is also a common practice. Furthermore, people may consult fortune tellers or Feng Shui experts to determine their compatibility with the current year's Tai Sui and receive personalized advice on how to navigate potential challenges and maximize positive outcomes.
keyMyths.source: DeBernardi, J. (2015). *The Way That Lives in the Heart: Chinese Religion and Popular Culture in Penang, Malaysia*.

sources.author: Henri Doré
sources.description: A comprehensive multi-volume work detailing various aspects of Chinese folk religion, including the Tai Sui.

sources.author: Stephan Feuchtwang
sources.work: Popular Religion in China: The Imperial Metaphor
sources.description: Examines the structure and function of popular religion in China, with insights into the role of deities like Tai Sui.
